---
title: "Visualization"
sidebar: tutorials
execute:
  eval: false
---

All nine visualization functions return a `plotnine.ggplot` object — composable
with `+` exactly like ggplot2 in R. No side effects: nothing is displayed until
you call `.draw()` or save with `.save()`.

```python
from yasqat.visualization import (
    index_plot, timeline_plot, spell_duration_plot,
    distribution_plot, entropy_plot, frequency_plot,
    modal_state_plot, mean_time_plot, parallel_coordinate_plot,
)
from plotnine import theme_bw, labs, theme, element_text
```

## Index plot

One row per sequence, one column per time step — coloured by state. The canonical
sequence visualization. Best for small to medium pools (< 500 sequences).

```python
p = index_plot(pool)
p.draw()

# Sorted by cluster assignment
p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels, sort_by_group=True)
p + theme_bw() + labs(title="Trajectories by cluster")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
`sort_by_group=True` stacks sequences by cluster, making within-cluster
homogeneity immediately visible. Combine with `group=` to add a cluster
boundary line between groups.
:::

## Distribution plot

State proportions at each time step — the cohort-level view. Stable bands
mean long spells; thin bands mean transient states.

```python
p = distribution_plot(pool)
p.draw()

# Proportion of sequences in each state over time
# Colour follows Alphabet.colors automatically
```

## Entropy plot

Cross-sectional Shannon entropy at each time point. Peaks signal moments of
maximum uncertainty in the cohort (e.g., at a policy change or economic shock).

```python
p = entropy_plot(pool)
p + labs(y="Shannon entropy", x="Time point")
```

## Frequency plot

The most common distinct sequences as horizontal bars. Useful for identifying
whether a few dominant patterns account for most of the cohort.

```python
p = frequency_plot(pool, n=20)   # top-20 most frequent sequences
p.draw()
```

## Modal state plot

At each time point, the most common (modal) state. A smooth signal of the
cohort's "central tendency" trajectory.

```python
p = modal_state_plot(pool)
p.draw()
```

## Mean time plot

Mean time spent in each state, as a bar chart — one bar per state.

```python
p = mean_time_plot(pool)
p + labs(title="Average time in state (all sequences)")
```

## Timeline plot

Horizontal Gantt-style bars — one row per sequence, coloured bars per spell.
Shows spell structure and duration directly.

```python
# Subset to first 50 sequences for readability
subset = pool[:50]
p = timeline_plot(subset)
p.draw()
```

## Spell duration plot

Distribution of spell lengths per state — how long do sequences stay in each
state before transitioning? Useful for identifying "sticky" vs transient states.

```python
p = spell_duration_plot(pool)
p + labs(x="Spell length (time steps)", y="State")
```

## Parallel coordinate plot

One line per sequence — each axis is a time step, the line traces the state
transitions. Works best with a coarse alphabet (≤ 6 states) and small pools.

```python
p = parallel_coordinate_plot(pool, alpha=0.1)   # alpha for line transparency
p.draw()
```

## Composing with plotnine

Every function returns a bare `ggplot` object. Apply any plotnine layer:

```python
from plotnine import theme_bw, theme, element_text, labs, scale_fill_manual

p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels)

p + theme_bw() \
  + theme(axis_text_y=element_text(size=7)) \
  + labs(title="Customer lifecycle trajectories", x="Day", y="Customer") \
  + scale_fill_manual(values=pool.alphabet.colors)
```

## Legend auto-suppression

When the alphabet has more than 15 states, legends are automatically hidden to
keep plots readable. All plot functions accept `show_legend`:

```python
# Auto (default) — hidden when >15 states, shown otherwise
p = index_plot(pool)

# Force legend on, even with many states
p = index_plot(pool, show_legend=True)

# Force legend off, even with few states
p = index_plot(pool, show_legend=False)
```

This applies to `index_plot`, `distribution_plot`, `timeline_plot`, and
`spell_duration_plot`.

## Saving plots

```python
p = index_plot(pool)
p.save("trajectories.png", dpi=150, width=12, height=8)
p.save("trajectories.pdf", width=12, height=8)
```

::: {.callout-note}
`plotnine` uses matplotlib as its rendering backend. For interactive display
in Jupyter, call `p.draw()`. In scripts, call `p.save()` or assign the output
of `p.draw()` to a variable to suppress auto-display.
:::
